authority_boundary: External eval systems inform Eval Forge archetype design. They are not OS Abyss proof authority, owner routing authority, or vendor lock-in.

sources.adopted: Start debugging agent behavior from traces that expose model calls, tool calls, guardrails, handoffs, routing changes, and regressions before hardening datasets.
sources.rejected: Hosted trace grading or eval runs cannot become central OS Abyss proof acceptance.

sources.source: OpenAI evaluation best practices and Evals deprecation boundary
sources.adopted: Use objective, representative data, metrics, run/compare loops, human calibration, and continuous eval growth.
sources.rejected: Do not couple durable OS Abyss proof control to deprecated hosted Evals platform semantics.

sources.source: Anthropic demystifying evals for AI agents
sources.adopted: Model agent evals as task, trials, graders, transcripts, environment outcomes, and a holistic signal stack including manual transcript review.
sources.rejected: Do not judge agentic behavior from final text alone when tools or state changes matter.

sources.adopted: Use deterministic trajectory matching when the expected route or tool-call sequence is the claim, and judge-based trajectory evaluation only when the valid path is flexible.
sources.rejected: Do not flatten deterministic route-law violations into broad LLM judge prompts.

sources.adopted: Manually review real traces before infrastructure, separate capability from regression evals, assign owner expertise, and choose run/trace/thread level deliberately.
sources.rejected: Do not automate mining before labels, reject taxonomy, owner gates, and error analysis exist.

sources.adopted: Coding agents require trajectory, trace/metadata assertions, cost/latency posture, repeated runs, and disposable workspaces for write-capable tests.
sources.rejected: Do not treat a coding agent as a one-shot text completion when file, shell, runtime, and state effects matter.

sources.source: Model Context Protocol security best practices
sources.adopted: MCP write-capable tools need explicit safety boundaries, least-privilege scope, consent posture, and validation; access-plane responses should make side effects visible.
sources.rejected: Do not let an MCP adapter become proof authority, arbitrary code execution, or a hidden write plane.

sources.adopted: Treat tools as potentially arbitrary code and require clear user/tool safety expectations before invocation.
sources.rejected: Do not rely on tool descriptions or generated readouts as trusted proof without owner validation.

sources.adopted: Use secure-by-default implementation rigor, practical validation tools, and deployment-specific threat controls for MCP in high-stakes agentic systems.
sources.rejected: Do not widen OS Abyss MCP writes without a new decision, validation route, and explicit proof-owner boundary.

sources.adopted: Represent future runnable evals as tasks with datasets, solvers or agents, tools, scorers, logs, and isolation boundaries.
sources.rejected: Do not run write-capable eval support without an explicit check, dry-run, or sandbox route.

sources.adopted: Separate task completion, tool correctness, argument correctness, and judge-based metrics so agent failures can be localized.
sources.rejected: Do not use a single score to hide whether reasoning, tool choice, arguments, or state outcome failed.

sources.adopted: Keep the data/task/scorer loop, immutable experiments, CI regression checks, production trace scoring, and feedback into datasets.
sources.rejected: Do not move OS Abyss proof truth into an external experiment dashboard.
